title: Foundations of Social Studies and Technical Vocational Education and Training (TVET)

units.title: Brief Introductory Notes on Social Studies: Meaning and Importance in National Development

sessions.title: The Meaning and Importance of Social Studies
indicators: Explain the meaning of Social Studies
Discuss any three importance of Social Studies in national development
sessions.content:
What is Social Studies?

Social Studies is the study of mankind and their relationship with their physical and social environment. It emphasises individual qualities and group ideals.

Key features:

  • Integrates ideas, facts, skills, and concepts from other subject areas, especially from social science subjects
  • Primary purpose: citizenship education
  • Helps learners understand and solve contemporary persistent issues of human survival
  • Equips learners with knowledge, skills, attitudes, and values needed to become informed citizens

Importance of Social Studies in National Development

1. Equips learners to become informed citizens

  • Provides knowledge of the constitution and constitutional systems of the nation
  • Prepares students to participate actively in constructive public action (e.g., registering to vote)
  • Inculcates positive attitudes and values such as honesty, hard work, patriotism, and tolerance
  • Helps learners avoid undesirable behaviors like stealing, corruption, laziness, absenteeism, and tribalism

2. Contributes through citizenship education

  • Citizenship education helps citizens understand and perform their responsibilities before enjoying their rights
  • Requires understanding the constitution and being committed to obeying laws
  • Develops loyalty to one's country and a sense of national consciousness

3. Equips learners with problem-solving skills

  • Develops social skills – getting along with others, building relationships
  • Develops conflict resolution skills – helping to resolve ethnic conflicts, chieftaincy disputes, and land litigation
  • Develops critical thinking skills – analyzing and evaluating information
  • Develops intellectual skills – reasoning and making informed decisions
  • Develops citizenship function skills – understanding civic responsibilities

4. Instills research and analytical skills

  • Helps students identify problems
  • Teaches gathering of data and analyzing data
  • Encourages students to examine and critique the existing social order and norms
  • Promotes social transformation by challenging dysfunctional ideas

5. Promotes healthy interaction and national unity

  • Develops positive attitudes and values that unite people
  • Teaches cooperation and how to make generalizations about human social behavior
  • Promotes social integration, equity, justice, and fair play
  • Develops national consciousness and unity among citizens

6. Prepares students for national development

  • Helps students understand how to contribute to their communities
  • Develops patriotism and love for the country
  • Teaches learners to care about the environment
  • Promotes responsible citizenship and national development

sessions.title: Understanding One Self
indicators: Distinguish between Self and Self-Concept
Discuss the different types of Self
sessions.content:
Meaning of Self

Self refers to the whole being of an individual, taking into consideration his/her personal and psychological nature and abilities.

What Self includes:

  • Memories, values, interests, and experiences
  • Esteem, goals, and ideas
  • Physical appearance and personality
  • Capabilities and weaknesses

Key point: Self explains how a person can change his/her weakness into positive achievements.

Meaning of Self-Concept

Self-Concept is a collection of beliefs about one's nature, qualities, and typical behavior. It is your mental picture of yourself.

Examples of self-concept beliefs:

  • 'I am easy-going'
  • 'I am pretty'
  • 'I am hardworking'

Key features of self-concept:

  • Refers to what a person believes to be true about himself/herself
  • Presents the total of a person's perception, beliefs, and feelings about themselves
  • Explains the individual in light of his/her potential (positive traits or attributes)
  • Helps individuals understand how their qualities can be developed

Important note: Some people pretend to be what they are not and want to show off to impress people. This is called False Identity.

Key Difference

  • Self = The totality of the individual (memories, values, interests, experiences, esteem, goals, ideas, and abilities)
  • Self-Concept = What a person believes to be true about himself/herself (the mental picture)

sessions.title: Types of Self
indicators: Mention the various types of self
Describe your present self
Mention what you hope to become in the future
sessions.content:
Introduction

The self of a person is dynamic as he/she interacts with the environment and relates with other people.

Types of Self

1. Real Self

  • Refers to the actual character, behavioural traits, and competencies that a person possesses
  • Describes the present state of an individual about his/her psychological nature and abilities
  • Answers the question: 'Who am I right now?'

2. Ideal Self

  • Refers to what a person would like to be in the future
  • Involves goal setting and personal development
  • Answers the question: 'Who would I like to become?'
  • Once you know what you want to become, you need to develop yourself well to achieve your dreams

3. Real Other Self

  • Refers to how a person thinks other people perceive him/her
  • Knowing your real other self helps you to reduce stress
  • Helps you develop self-confidence and reduce the stress of getting stuck in your perception of how others think of you
  • Helps you to adapt to challenging situations (e.g., managing anger and other inadequacies)
  • Answers the question: 'How do others see me?'

4. Ideal Other Self

  • Refers to the expectations and standards that other people have for the individual
  • Helps individuals deal with conflicting expectations (e.g., parents wanting you to become a doctor while you prefer teaching)
  • Answers the question: 'How would others like me to be?'

Example of handling conflict: If your parents want you to become a medical doctor but you want to become a teacher, you can engage them positively by explaining your passion and showing how teaching contributes to national development.

sessions.title: Psychological Dimensions of Self-Concept
indicators: Explain the terms: Self-Identity, Self-Esteem, and Self-Efficacy
Discuss the importance of Self-Identity
sessions.content:
Self-Identity

Self-Identity is composed of relatively permanent self-assessments that make an individual unique and distinct from all other individuals.

What Self-Identity includes:

  • Personality attributes – e.g., being thoughtful, quiet, talkative, or easily upset
  • Knowledge of one's skills and abilities
  • Occupation and hobbies
  • Awareness of physical attributes – e.g., 'I am tall'
  • Values, beliefs, and aspirations of the person

Important distinction: Temporary states like 'I am tired' are not part of self-identity because they are not permanent.

Importance of Self-Identity

1. To develop positive qualities

  • Knowing yourself helps you identify and develop your potential
  • The ability to achieve depends on how the person looks at himself/herself
  • A person who believes they have positive qualities is likely to be successful

2. To identify weaknesses

  • Objective assessment of characteristics helps reveal weaknesses
  • This diagnosis helps find ways to overcome shortcomings
  • Knowing oneself helps a person live just the way he/she is

3. To take the right decision at the right time

  • Helps individuals make good choices based on their strengths
  • Enables tapping of qualities for personal benefit and national development
  • Makes the person self-reliant, confident, independent, and responsible

4. To develop good interpersonal relations

  • Knowledge of one's personality type (introvert, extrovert, sanguine, melancholy) helps in developing and managing relationships
  • Extroverts are sociable and friendly
  • Introverts are reserved and keep to themselves
  • Sanguine people are easy to approach
  • Melancholy people are always sad
  • Knowing these categories helps relate to other people in the right way, promoting peace, love, and understanding

5. To appreciate other people's views

  • Helps individuals accept and appreciate what people say about them
  • If people talk about a person's negative attitudes, awareness helps them take remedial measures

Self-Esteem

Self-Esteem refers to the extent to which we like, accept, or approve of ourselves – how much we value ourselves.

High Self-Esteem leads to:

  • Confidence in our abilities
  • Self-acceptance
  • Not worrying about what others think about us
  • Optimism

Low Self-Esteem leads to:

  • Lack of confidence
  • Wanting to be or look like others
  • Always worrying about what others might think
  • Pessimism

Factors influencing Self-Esteem:

  • Reaction of others: If people admire us, our self-esteem is high; if they despise us, it grows low
  • Comparison with others: Comparing with people who are better off lowers self-esteem; comparing with less successful people raises it
  • Social roles: Some roles (doctor, teacher, lawyer) carry prestige; others carry stigma

Self-Efficacy

Self-Efficacy is a person's belief about his/her chances of accomplishing a specific task.

Key points:

  • There is a strong linkage between high self-efficacy expectations and success rate
  • Low self-efficacy expectation tends to have low success rates
  • The self-efficacy theory holds that people program themselves for success or failure by enacting their self-efficacy expectations
  • Positive or negative results subsequently become feedback for one's personal experience and thinking

sessions.title: The Concept of Personality
indicators: Explain the concept of personality
Mention some personality traits and explain them
sessions.content:
Concept of Personality

Personality refers to the qualities or attributes of an individual that makes him or her different from others. These attributes or qualities are unique to the individual.

Key point: Even identical twins may possess one or two traits that make it possible to differentiate between them. For example, no two persons have the same thumb prints.

Attributes of Human Personality

1. Name

  • All human beings have names that identify them
  • Names do not make individuals unique because they may be shared by many people

2. Physical Appearance

  • Describes personality in terms of height, size, and shape
  • Whether the person is tall, short, fat, slim, or timid-looking
  • Even with twins, there may be minor differences in personal outlook

3. Extrovert

  • A lively and confident person who enjoys the company of other people
  • Likes working in groups
  • Wants to be seen and heard everywhere they are
  • Can easily make friends and are usually kind
  • Are outspoken and do not easily become angry

4. Introvert

  • The direct opposite of an extrovert
  • Always reserved and enjoy working alone
  • In the classroom, introverts hardly raise their hands to ask or answer questions
  • May feel shy, inferior, and lack self-confidence
  • They prefer to listen to others without allowing others the opportunity to listen to their views

5. Temperament

  • An individual's level of emotional response
  • People with bad temperament easily become angry and lose control
  • People with good temperament can control their emotions and anger in the face of provocation

Types of Temperament:

a) Choleric Temperament

  • Typically extroverted, goal-oriented, and ambitious
  • Many are natural-born leaders
  • Can be short-tempered and even violent

b) Sanguine Temperament

  • Naturally people-oriented
  • Has an active, positive movement in a favourable environment
  • Influences their environment by encouraging others to work together
  • Also extroverted, fun-loving, playful, impulsive, persuasive, easily amused, and optimistic
  • Builds relationships quickly and very easily

c) Phlegmatic Temperament

  • Naturally service-oriented
  • Passive, introverted, calm, unemotional, easy-going, indecisive, patient, and slow when responding to other people
  • Strongly resists change and needs time to adjust
  • They are consistent and persistent in whatever they undertake

d) Melancholy Temperament

  • Naturally quality-oriented and wants to do things right
  • Reserved and suspicious until they probe and are sure of your intentions
  • They fear risk-taking and making wrong decisions
  • Exhibit great sadness or depression; they have mood swings

sessions.title: Human Capabilities
indicators: Explain Human Capabilities
Discuss at least any four ways by which the individual can develop his/her capabilities
sessions.content:
Concept of Human Capabilities

Capabilities are the inert potentials to perform certain actions or achieve certain outcomes. Human Capabilities refer to what we can do.

What capabilities include:

  • The ability to think
  • The ability to remember
  • The ability to create
  • The ability to invent
  • The ability to control the environment
  • The ability to solve problems

Key point: All human beings have unique capabilities. Some may be able to sing, write, design, or invent.

How an Individual Can Develop His/Her Capabilities

1. Education and Training

  • Develops one's intellect
  • Equips individuals with skills
  • Government should ensure free and compulsory basic education for all school-going age children

2. Role Taking

  • Individuals can take up leadership roles in communal labour, sports and games, and investment promotion activities
  • This helps improve socio-economic conditions of people in the community

3. Taking an Identity

  • A person needs to take a family name by which he/she will be identified
  • This gives assurance and backing from family members
  • Builds self-confidence for whatever activities one ventures into

4. Self-Determination

  • An individual can develop capabilities by setting a target in life and deciding to achieve it at all costs
  • Setting a goal makes one put in all efforts to achieve it

5. Provision of a Conducive Home Environment

  • Parents and siblings should ensure a conducive environment at home
  • Love and affection are needed to develop one's capabilities

6. Self-Awareness and Knowledge of Capabilities

  • Becoming aware of yourself and your capabilities helps you:
    • Take the right steps in life
    • Make the right decisions when choosing a career or a trade

Importance of Identifying Capabilities

1. To set realistic goals

  • Knowledge of capabilities tells what you can do
  • Helps you know what to plan your life around
  • Helps you set short-term, medium-term, and long-term goals

2. To avoid disappointment

  • Knowing your capabilities helps avoid setting unrealistic goals
  • Setting unrealistic goals leads to non-achievement and disappointment in life

3. To develop self-confidence

  • Knowing your capabilities helps you know who you are and what you can do
  • Helps you choose a career or future vocation
  • Helps you identify your strengths and weaknesses

4. For future development of self

  • It is important to know your capabilities so that you can develop yourself further in areas where you think you are weak

units.title: Links and Connections Between the Individual, Family, School and Community

sessions.title: The Family Systems
indicators: Explain the concept of family
Explain the types of family systems
sessions.content:
Meaning of Family

A family is a group of people united by ties of marriage, blood, or adoption. People become members of a family through blood, affinity (marriage), or adoption.

Key point: When one becomes a member of a family through any of these means, he or she does so because of kinship ties.

Types of Family Systems

1. Nuclear Family

  • Limited to parents and immediate offspring (father, mother, and children)
  • Children may be biological or adopted
  • Known as the primary or restricted family
  • Relations: spouses (husband and wife), daughters, sisters, brothers, sons, mothers, and fathers
  • Consists of two generations: first generation (mother and father) and second generation (children)
  • Other names: natural family, primary family, restricted family, and immediate family
  • Family of orientation: The family into which a person is born (self, siblings, and parents)
  • Family of procreation: The family created through marriage (self, spouse, and children)

2. Extended Family

  • Operates in traditional African societies
  • Belief: when a man and a woman marry, they do so into families
  • Members include: father, mother, children, mother's parent, father's parents, mother's sisters, and father's sisters
  • Relations: nephew, niece, mother, father, uncle, cousin, aunt, grandfathers, grandmothers, grandsons, granddaughters, etc.
  • Consists of three or four generations
  • Most families in Ghana are extended families, especially in rural areas

3. Single Parent Family

  • Either the man or woman takes the responsibilities of both father and mother
  • Members include: father or mother and child/children
  • Relations: father/mother, sister(s), and brother(s)

4. Blended Family

  • Consists of parents, one or both of whom have children from a previous relationship
  • Each becomes a step-parent to the other's children
  • Children become step-brothers or step-sisters
  • If parents have a child of their own, children from previous marriage become half-brothers and half-sisters to the new child

5. Dyed Family

  • Consists of only the father and the mother (no children)

sessions.title: The Merits and Demerits of the Family Systems
indicators: Discuss the merits and demerits of the various family types
sessions.content:
Merits of the Nuclear Family

  • Two people share the responsibility of parenting
  • Parents have more time to spend with their children
  • No interference in family affairs from external persons
  • Little or no conflict when it comes to inheritance
  • Can share financial responsibilities if both parents work
  • Income may be sufficient since there are few mouths to feed

Demerits of the Nuclear Family

  • If incomes are insufficient, there could be a vicious cycle of poverty with no one to rely on
  • Children whose parents are dead may suffer in terms of upbringing
  • In traditional African setting, a large number of children is regarded as prestige – nuclear family is at a disadvantage
  • Death means extinction of the family if children die and parents do not adopt
  • Difficult for relatives to offer support in times of difficulty since they are under no obligation to do so

Merits of the Single-Parent Family

  • Children often participate in more household tasks and take on responsibilities at an early age
  • Helps them to be responsible, dependable, and independent
  • Dedicated single parents can provide a high level of care, security, and love
  • Children can have happy, full lives due to love and security from their sole parent

Demerits of the Single-Parent Family

  • Parenting is a big job to be done alone
  • Single parents must balance all responsibilities of parenting, managing a household, and holding a job
  • No one to share the challenges or problems of rearing children
  • Usually have less time to spend on their interests
  • Many single parents are responsible for the family's expenses and do not receive financial support from the child's other parent
  • Single mothers often have financial problems because men generally have higher-paying jobs

Merits of the Extended Family

  • Sees to the upbringing of children in the family
  • Provides the basis of moral support to family members (aged, disease, mishaps, etc.)
  • Provides economic support to members (e.g., employment)
  • Maintains the cultural heritage of the family
  • Gives identity and a sense of belongingness to its members

Demerits of the Extended Family

  • Reliance on relatives for future property leads to sheer laziness
  • Hindrance to development – adhering to old laws and customs may prevent land release for development
  • Family interferes in personal affairs like marriage
  • Becomes a burden to individuals and income earners
  • Conflicts may arise as to how to divide household responsibilities

sessions.title: Functions of the Family
indicators: Mention and explain some functions of the family
Discuss some specific roles of individual members of the family
sessions.content:
Primary Functions of the Family

1. Procreation

  • The family reproduces to replace dead members of society
  • Ensures the continuity of society

2. Provision of Physical Needs

  • Provides shelter, food, clothing and other material needs of members

3. Transmission of Culture

  • The family transmits society's culture to the child
  • The child learns how to interact and work with others and fit into the community

4. Status and Identity

  • The family gives status and identity to its members
  • Every child is given a name that gives them their identity

Secondary Functions of the Family

1. Religion and Moral Training

  • Provides basic religion and moral training to members
  • Examples: baptism, ordination, comfort, and moral support

2. Health Needs

  • Provides the health needs of members
  • Members are obliged to protect each other from diseases
  • Care for the unfortunate ones in times of sickness

3. Education

  • Role of family to provide education to relatives
  • A long-time investment for progress and development of the entire family

4. Economic Unit

  • Serves as an economic unit by opening shops for the entire family

sessions.title: Roles of Family Members
indicators: Discuss the specific roles of members of the family
Discuss the reasons for the changing roles of members of the family
sessions.content:
Roles of the Husband/Father

1. Physical Maintenance (Breadwinner)

  • Responsible for providing money for food, clothing, shelter, school fees, water bills, electricity bills
  • Takes care of the health needs of family members
  • A father who fails to provide basic needs is considered irresponsible

2. Disciplinarian

  • Educates family members to know what is expected of them
  • Sets the rules and ensures they are obeyed
  • Serves as a role model

3. Provision of Education

  • Ensures family members have access to formal education including vocational training and apprenticeship

4. Sexual Satisfaction

  • Ensures procreation takes place
  • Provides the wife with sexual and emotional needs

5. Major Decision Making

  • Expected to make major decisions that affect the management of the home
  • Takes the final decision in the home (e.g., settlement of conflicts)

Roles of the Wife/Mother

1. House Care

  • Cares for family members in areas of cleanliness, washing of clothes, and training in house chores

2. Socialization Role

  • Plays an active role in the socialization of young members of the family

3. Management of Resources

  • Manages all resources at home to ensure the well-being of the whole family
  • Purchases food items, cooks, and plans the home with the father's financial resources

4. Sexual Satisfaction

  • Plays a role in procreation
  • Carries the fetus in her womb for nine months before delivery

5. Income Generating Ventures

  • Supplements the husband's income by engaging in economic activities
  • Shows unconditional love towards her children
  • Trains children, especially daughters, about dangers of teenage pregnancy

Roles of Children

  • Show respect toward their parents
  • Support their parents in all household chores (cooking, washing utensils, fetching water, scrubbing, running errands)
  • Provide economic support to their parents after school/studies
  • Be obedient to their parents
  • Show love towards their parents
  • Are a source of cheap labour, especially in traditional communities in Ghana

Changing Roles of Family Members

1. Education

  • Women are now in paid employment
  • Men can now feature prominently in the kitchen and market
  • In some situations, men work to supplement the role of the wife

2. Economic/Financial Constraints

  • Due to economic hardship and high cost of living, women work to support the household budget
  • The wife may assist the husband to pay electricity and water bills and other domestic expenses

sessions.title: The Concept of Socialisation
indicators: Define socialization
List the agents of socialization
Explain the socialization process
sessions.content:
Meaning of Socialization

Socialization is the process of inducting children into the ways of life of their society. It is the conscious effort to pass on the culture of the society to the individual.

Key definitions:

  • The process of social interaction through which people acquire personality and learn the way of life of their society
  • The process whereby people learn the attitudes, values, and actions appropriate to individual learning about the culture of society
  • An inter-generational transfer of values, norms, knowledge, and perceptions from an older generation to the new generation

How Socialization Takes Place

1. Observation

  • The child observes what the parent does
  • Sits by and goes on errands for the father or mother
  • Learns the art of the work, materials used, and when and how things are done

2. Participation

  • Children participate in the economic activities of parents or relatives
  • In coastal societies, boys accompany fathers to sea; girls help mothers in processing fish
  • In farming areas, children help with farm work
  • In Kente weaving areas, young boys are asked to sit on the loom to weave

3. Education

  • The child learns the way of life of society either informally or formally

Agents/Agencies of Socialization

1. Home

  • The home is the first agency of socialization
  • The family socializes the individual either deliberately or unconsciously
  • Provides emotional affection, love, language, norms, and values of society

2. The School

  • Formally socializes the child in a structured environment
  • Charged with training children to acquire knowledge, skills, and values
  • Under the direct supervision of people who are not relatives (teachers)

3. Mass Media

  • Various forms such as print and electronic reach a large audience
  • Includes newspapers, magazines, books, television, radio, movies, videos, and records
  • Helps to transmit cultural knowledge, rules, moral behaviour, and other information

4. Church/Mosque

  • Through religious doctrines, helps to shape the attitudes of the child
  • Teaches religious ethics and discipline that help mould the child to become a responsible citizen

5. Peer Group

  • Mobilizes themselves for communal work
  • Shares the hopes, problems, and aspirations of the group
  • Has rules and regulations that one needs to obey to continually enjoy membership

sessions.title: The Impact of the Family, School, and Community on the Development and Training of the Individual
indicators: Explain the links and connections between the individual, family, school, and community
sessions.content:
The Family

The family is the first social and psychological environment in which the child is born.

Functions of the family in socialization:

  • Procreation is the responsibility of parents
  • Functions start immediately after birth with feeding, bathing, etiquette, toilet rules, and economic activities
  • In nuclear families, parents may transfer some functions to senior siblings
  • In extended families, a child receives care, sanctions, or instructions from any close relative
  • If one misbehaves, they are reprimanded or beaten by a close relative who happens to be at the scene

The School

  • Children meet unfamiliar people and have new experiences
  • Learn to interact with people who may have social backgrounds different from their own
  • Teaches a wide range of knowledge and skills that society cherishes
  • Clubs and societies help children learn leadership roles and obeying rules and regulations
  • The prefect's system helps to socialize children into future political life
  • Contestants learn to appreciate success as well as defeat

The Community

  • Made up of individual families in the area
  • Social cohesion is very strong in villages and cottages
  • The local community operates in the same way as extended family members
  • Court of public opinion helps the development of the individual
  • Our attitudes and behaviours are influenced by what we perceive to be the opinion of other members of our society about ourselves
  • People often try to conform to the attitudes of others to avoid being singled out as a different person

units.title: The Concept of Technical, Vocational Education and Training (TVET)

sessions.title: The Meaning of TVET
indicators: Explain the concept of Technical, Vocational Education and Training (TVET)
Discuss the importance of TVET
sessions.content:
Definition of TVET

Technical, Vocational Education and Training (TVET) is an educational program designed to provide knowledge and skills needed at the workplace for specific industries, trades, and occupations.

Key definitions:

  • UNESCO and ILO definition: TVET refers to aspects of the educational process involving, in addition to general education, the study of technologies and related sciences and the acquisition of practical skills, attitudes, understanding, and knowledge relating to occupations in various sectors of economic and social life
  • TVET is a complex and multi-dimensional field whose principal objective is to train people and prepare them for the labour market

Components of TVET:

  • Technical aspect: Equips individuals with practical knowledge and skills for the manipulation of physical instruments in doing projects
  • Vocational aspect: Prepares the individual for various jobs and occupations by combining theory and practice
  • Training aspect: Geared towards the acquisition of specific skills for specific jobs

Importance of TVET in Ghana's Socio-Economic Development

1. Provides needed skilled manpower

  • The unemployment rate among youth is higher than that of adults
  • Ghana's educational system puts major emphasis on academic subjects with little attention on technical and vocational education
  • TVET will prepare individuals for the job market by equipping them with occupational skills necessary for working in industry

2. Provides relevant employment skills

  • The economic competitiveness of a country depends on the skills of its workforce
  • TVET is perceived as a crucial element in enhancing economic productivity
  • The unemployment situation has its root cause in the bookish nature of the current educational system
  • TVET will prepare individuals with the requisite and relevant skills needed to gain employment in particular sectors

3. Helps government receive more taxes

  • Many youth who have gone to school are not working and therefore not paying taxes
  • Shifting attention to vocational education gets the youth into self-employed initiatives
  • This can contribute significantly to the economic development of Ghana

4. Makes the country a haven for investment

  • When people are working, it reduces crime rates such as armed robbery
  • Paves the way for investors to do business in the country
  • TVET enables students to develop a good mindset towards manual work
  • Prepares school leavers to acquire entrepreneurial and productive skills

sessions.title: The Four Main Areas of TVET
indicators: List the four main areas of TVET
Briefly explain the four areas of TVET
sessions.content:
The Four Main Areas of TVET

1. Technical Skills

  • The application of scientific principles to equip the individual with skills, attitudes, values, knowledge, and understanding
  • Enables the individual to function in the world of work
  • Practical and require the manipulation of tools to perform a specific task

2. Visual Arts

  • Pieces of art that create or stimulate us through a visual experience
  • When we look at them, they often provoke a feeling of some sort within the individual

3. Home Economics

  • The study of human and material forces affecting homes and families
  • The utilization of this knowledge for the benefit of mankind
  • A field of study that deals with relationships between individuals, families, communities, and the environment in which they live
  • Draws knowledge from various disciplines and uses the knowledge as an integrated body to solve individual and family problems

4. Agriculture Science

  • The art, science, and business of cultivating crops and livestock for economic purposes
  • The cultivation of crops and the rearing of farm animals for human use
  • The disposal of these products through marketing

sessions.title: The Concept of Technical Skills
indicators: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the branches of Technical Skills
Discuss any two important Technical Skills in national development
sessions.content:
Branches of Technical Skills

1. Wood Technology

  • The trade or skills of using wood and wood products in making artefacts
  • Involves furniture making or interior fittings

2. Metal Technology

  • The process of working with metals to create or assemble individual parts of items or large-scale structures
  • Includes fitting and machining, fabrication and welding, foundry, and forging
  • Deals with the construction, building, manufacturing, and production of mechanical parts and articles

3. Automotive Technology

  • The study of engine construction, fuel and ignition systems, power trains, brakes, transmissions, electronics, and diagnostic equipment
  • An auto mechanic performs maintenance, diagnostic testing, repairs, and inspections of small trucks and cars
  • Deals with everything about vehicles – heavy and light trucks, cars, etc.
  • Involves the manufacture, operation, design, and sale of motor vehicles and their parts

4. Electrical Technology

  • A trade that deals with installing, maintaining, and repairing electrical control, wiring, and lighting systems
  • Involves the flow of current or electrons through metals and conductors such as machines, tools, devices, and systems

5. Electronic Technology

  • Involves the designing and testing of circuits that use the electromagnetic properties of electrical components
  • Components include: resistors, capacitors, diodes, transistors, and inductors
  • Deals with the designing, creation, repair, and development of everyday devices such as mobile phones, portable music devices, television, and computers

Importance of Technical Skills

  • Creates employment opportunities for people
  • Improves the quality of living standard by producing trained and experienced manpower
  • Increases productivity – makes work processes easy and faster
  • Reduces poverty – people who engage in technical skills earn income to improve their standard of living
  • Enhances economic, social, and industrial development (construction of roads, industries, hospitals, and schools)
  • Creates employable skills for the job market or self-employment

sessions.title: The Concept of Visual Arts
indicators: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the branches of Visual Arts
Explain at least any two importance of Visual Arts
sessions.content:
Concept of Visual Arts

Visual Arts involves the creation of symbolic, artistic, and creative expressions using visual imagery to satisfy human needs.

Key points:

  • Involves nature and man's ability to capture the moment on a piece of paper
  • Common visual arts include: Picture Making, Ceramics and Pottery, Sculpture, Textiles, Jewellery, Leatherwork, Graphic Design
  • They can be seen and touched

Branches of Visual Arts

1. Picture Making

  • A two-dimensional (2-D) art in the form of pictures
  • Includes: drawing and colouring, painting, collage, mosaic, photomontage, photography, and other digital imagery
  • Consists of drawing lines and shapes, painting, and making prints on surfaces to show places, people, scenes, and things

2. Ceramics and Pottery

  • Ceramics: Things made from materials that are permanently changed when heated (e.g., tableware, tiles)
  • Pottery: Containers produced with clay
  • Examples: earthen bowls, mugs, flower vases

3. Sculpture

  • An artistic form in which hard or plastic materials are worked into three-dimensional art objects
  • Materials: wood, plaster, fabric, metal, clay, rubber, etc.
  • Can be moulded, carved, sewn, cast, wrought, welded, or assembled
  • Examples: statues, carved objects, assemblage and construction items

4. Graphic Design

  • A two-dimensional art made by combining letters, symbols, words, pictures, and decorations as a means of communicating
  • Examples: posters, banners, flyers, billboards, magazines
  • The art or skill of combining text and pictures in advertisements, magazines, or books

5. Leatherwork

  • The making of items from leather such as bags, belts, shoes, etc.
  • Involves: designing, cutting, hole punching, sewing, finishing, decorating, carving, and dyeing
  • Includes the art of preparing animal skins and hides into leather

6. Jewellery

  • Designing and making small decorative items worn for personal adornment
  • Examples: broaches, rings, necklaces, earrings, cufflinks

7. Textiles

  • The study of chemicals, polymers, and fibres
  • Technology used for developing and creating fabrics, textiles, and garments from yarn
  • Fabrics are produced by weaving, printing, knotting, crocheting, etc.

Importance of Visual Arts

  • Serves as a source of employment and income earning
  • Beautifies the environment
  • Helps students improve their mental and imagination skills
  • Gives us joy and is sometimes used as therapy
  • Improves our creative skills
  • Helps us to communicate with one another
  • Helps in the expression of emotions
  • Helps to improve one's confidence level
  • Helps to preserve, sustain, and transmit cultures

sessions.title: Concept of Home Economics
indicators: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the concept of Home Economics
Explain at least any two importance of Home Economics
sessions.content:
Concept of Home Economics

Home Economics is a field of study that deals with the relationship between individuals, families, communities, and the environment in which they live.

Key points:

  • Draws knowledge from various disciplines and uses the knowledge in an integrated manner to solve individual and family problems
  • Defined as: the study of human and material factors affecting homes and families and the utilization of this knowledge for the benefit of mankind

Branches of Home Economics

1. Clothing and Textiles

  • The scientific study of the production and use of textile fibres and fabrics
  • Clothing aspect: Clothing production, good grooming, and clothing management
  • Textile aspect: Production of raw textile fibres, yarns, fabrics, and finishing processes

2. Food and Nutrition

  • Food: Any substance which nourishes the body when eaten or drunk
  • Nutrition: The discipline or science which investigates the nutrients derived from food and how it affects the body
  • Studies the relationship between food and the body and sometimes human behaviours related to food
  • Seeks to prevent diseases and promote health through the intake of balanced and safe foods

3. Management in Living

  • Deals with family and its composition, decision-making, care of the house, housing, and financial management
  • The application of management principles to everyday living
  • Involves decisions individuals and families make as they go through their lives to achieve goals

Importance of Home Economics

  • Helps to improve the quality of life of people through the family
  • Equips the individual with coping strategies to live well in his/her environment
  • Shapes people's personalities, perspectives, and views of life
  • Fosters nation-building through the provision of balanced and nutritious meals
  • Raises the standard of living by providing a source of livelihood
  • Promotes practical skills for daily living (decision-making, credit management, meal planning, clothing management)
  • Enhances productivity by training people with employable skills
  • Reduces the burden of unemployment – most skills prepare people to be self-employed

sessions.title: The Concept of Agriculture Science
indicators: Explain the concept of Agriculture and discuss at least any two branches
Explain at least any two importance of Agriculture
sessions.content:
Concept of Agriculture Science

Agriculture Science is defined as the science or practice of farming, including cultivation of the soil for the growing of crops and the rearing of animals to provide food, wool, and other products.

Key points:

  • Includes cultivation of crops and rearing of farm animals
  • Involves disposal of products through marketing

Branches of Agriculture

1. Crop Science

  • Deals with the study of crops, their management, harvesting, and storage
  • Includes planting, harvesting, food processing, and storage
  • Ensures crops perform well for a good return in terms of quantity and quality

2. Animal Science

  • Concerned with the rearing and management of farm animals
  • Involves raising animals for meat, milk, eggs, fibre, and other products

3. Soil Science

  • The study of classification, survey, conservation, and management of soil for crop and animal production

4. Fish Farming (Fisheries)

  • A form of aquaculture in which fish is raised in enclosures or tanks
  • Involves study of wildlife resources in the aquatic ecosystem

5. Horticulture and Landscape Design

  • The art or practice of garden cultivation and management
  • Deals with the study of the cultivation of fruits, vegetables, and ornamental plants

6. Agricultural Mechanization

  • Deals with the study and operations of agricultural machinery, tools, and equipment
  • Involves using machines to work on land to increase farm worker productivity

7. Forestry

  • The science that deals with the study of forest resources, their management, and conservation

8. Agricultural Economics

  • A study and the application of economic principles in agriculture

9. Agronomy

  • The study of the inter-relationship and inter-dependencies of crops and soils

10. Pathology

  • The study of diseases affecting plants and animals and their control

11. Entomology

  • The study of insect pests and their management

Importance of Agriculture

  • Provides employment and serves as a source of livelihood
  • Supplies food to humans as well as fodder for domestic animals
  • Provides fibres that are used for clothes and furnishings in the home
  • Contributes to the country's revenue and serves as an important source of resource mobilization
  • Ensures food security in a country
  • Serves as a source of raw material for major industries (textiles, food processing, pharmaceutical industries)
  • Serves as a major source of foreign exchange for most developing countries
  • Is a source of drug and medicine production

units.title: Interrelatedness Between Technical, Vocational Education and Training and Social Studies

sessions.title: Interrelatedness Between Technical Skills and Social Studies
indicators: Explain the relationship between Technical Skills and Social Studies
sessions.content:
Definitions

Social Studies: The study of mankind and its relationship in both social and physical environments. It deals with the individual, the family, and the community.

Technical Skills: The knowledge and expertise needed to accomplish complex tasks.

Relationship Between Technical Skills and Social Studies

1. Technical skills facilitate teaching and learning of Social Studies

  • Technical knowledge is employed to produce Teaching and Learning Materials (TLMs)
  • These materials motivate, support, and promote effective teaching of Social Studies in schools

2. Both prepare individuals for responsible citizenship

  • Social Studies aims to prepare individuals to be competent, reflective, participatory, and responsible citizens
  • Technical Skills help achieve this dream by equipping individuals with skills needed in every aspect of life
  • Makes life easy and comfortable and helps one earn a living

3. Technical skills provide employment avenues

  • Helps individuals to earn a living
  • Example: Technical skills produce laboratory technicians who are good in electronics and help detect ailments in hospitals

4. Technical skills help prevent social vices

  • Skills acquired engage individuals in productive work
  • Keeps them away from bad behaviours like armed robbery, drug abuse, and prostitution
  • Brings about peace and harmonious living in the community

5. Technical skills equip individuals to provide infrastructure

  • Masons build houses; auto electricians work on cars
  • Individuals with relevant knowledge and skills work in industries to produce standardized goods
  • Makes life comfortable for community members

sessions.title: Interrelatedness Between Visual Arts and Social Studies
indicators: Explain the relationship between Visual Arts and Social Studies
sessions.content:
Definitions

Visual Arts: Involves the creation of symbolic and artistic creative expressions using visual imagery to satisfy human needs.

Social Studies: The study of mankind and its relationship in both social and physical environments. It deals with the individual, the family, and the community.

Relationship Between Visual Arts and Social Studies

1. Both help identify and solve societal problems

  • Visual arts and Social Studies have a strong influence on the development of society
  • Visual arts provide pleasure and creative inspiration
  • Together, they help foster dialogue and bring important issues to public view
  • Example: A photojournalist's image of famine in Sudan brought international attention and help to the victim

2. Cultural artefacts portray society's culture

  • Visual arts create artefacts that portray the culture or way of life of a particular group
  • Society depends on the products of visual arts for survival in everyday activities
  • Examples: Clothes, footwear, cooking pots, mugs

3. Both enhance national development

  • Both tell people about their cultural heritage
  • People gain understanding of having common ancestors
  • They tend to relate well with each other and fight for a common goal
  • Art can bring people together to work with a common vision

4. Visual arts provide employment opportunities

  • Helps individuals to earn a decent living
  • Helps eliminate insecurity and social vices in society

5. Both promote civilization and modernization

  • Due to technological and artistic advancement of people
  • Leads to the modernization of cultures, societies, and countries

sessions.title: Interrelatedness of Home Economics and Social Studies
indicators: Explain the relationship between Home Economics and Social Studies
sessions.content:
Definitions

Home Economics: A field of study that deals with the relationship between individuals, families, communities, and the environment in which they live. Draws knowledge from various disciplines to solve individual and family problems.

Social Studies: A course of study that deals with human relationships and the way society works.

Relationship Between Home Economics and Social Studies

1. Both consider human relationships and peaceful living

  • Primary focus of Social Studies is the individual, family, and community
  • Home Economics deals with relationships between individuals, families, communities, and the environment
  • Both help to improve the quality of life of people through the family
  • When families are strengthened, the whole society is empowered

2. Both deal with interdependence between individuals and their environment

  • Home Economics empowers individuals with basic and complex skills of life
  • Enables them to become effective and efficient users of environmental resources
  • Empowers learners with basic life skills such as personal hygiene for sustainable health
  • Both cater to the environment and enhance individuals' capacity to adopt environmentally friendly practices

3. Both focus on society through individuals and families

  • Home Economics is concerned with the holistic nature of humans (physical, biological, social, intellectual, aesthetic, and psychological well-being)
  • Social Studies looks at the relationship between human beings and how society works
  • Both seek to make learners tolerant and maintain inter-group understanding

4. Both satisfy needs and solve problems of society

  • Food is needed by every human being to think rationally
  • Home Economics educates individuals on how, when, and where to choose, prepare, and eat healthy food
  • Helps reduce stress and creates an enabling environment for individuals and families
  • Both promote environmental hygiene, prevent diseases, and promote good health

5. Both prepare individuals psychologically and emotionally

  • Management in Family Living (an aspect of Home Economics) prepares individuals in:
    • Effective communication
    • Family cohesion
    • Decision-making
    • Financial management
    • Conflict management
  • These are also important in Social Studies for preparing individuals to become humane, rational, and independent

sessions.title: Interrelatedness of Agriculture Science and Social Studies
indicators: Explain the relationship between Agriculture Science and Social Studies
sessions.content:
Definitions

Agriculture Science: The science or practice of farming, including cultivation of the soil for growing crops and rearing of animals to provide food, wool, and other products.

Social Studies: The study of mankind and its relationship in both social and physical environments. It deals with the individual, the family, and the community.

Relationship Between Agriculture Science and Social Studies

1. Agriculture provides food for a healthy community

  • The types of crops cultivated determine the staple food of a group of people
  • This is an aspect of culture in Social Studies
  • Ghanaian society is said to be agrarian – the majority of people are involved in agriculture

2. Agriculture helps reduce crime and prevent social vices

  • Provides jobs to the youth
  • Career opportunities in agriculture include:
    • Agricultural production, Agribusiness, Agricultural product processing and marketing
    • Agricultural extension workers, Agronomists, Biotechnologists
    • Animal technicians, Breeding supervisors, Livestock farm managers
    • Veterinarians, Zoo keepers, Animal Health Officers
    • Soil conservationists, Soil scientists, Teachers

3. Culture determines the farming system

  • The culture of a group of people determines the type of crops and animals cultivated and reared
  • In areas where animal rearing is common, animals are used as dowry
  • Festivals are normally celebrated to appreciate a bumper harvest

4. Agriculture helps beautify the environment

  • Horticulture and growing ornamental crops
  • Trees purify the air and make it safe for breathing
  • Ensures environmental sustainability – a prime focus of Social Studies
  • Agriculture provides an ideal area for individuals to acquire understanding of environmental concepts and develop skills and attitudes

sessions.title: Implications for Teaching TVET Courses in the Basic Schools
indicators: Explain the implications of integrating the various TVET courses into Basic Education
sessions.content:
Introduction

Teaching TVET courses at the basic levels of education is of great importance to the learner and teacher, thus improving teaching and learning in our schools.

Implications for Teaching TVET Courses

1. Visual Arts in Basic Schools

  • Enables the child to develop and improve motor skills
  • Art enables students to use tools and equipment (pencils, brushes, colours) to develop skills of working with their hands
  • Improves the creative skills of children
  • Enables children to see the integrated nature of subjects in the basic school curriculum

2. Home Economics in Basic Schools

  • Helps children acquire basic and complex skills of life
  • Basic life skills such as personal hygiene for sustainable health throughout life
  • Gives people the opportunity to pursue higher education and vocational training
  • Teaches children important life skills: cooking, sewing, and resource management

3. Agriculture in Basic Schools

  • Helps children receive formal education and training
  • Provides career opportunities: Agricultural Researchers, Agricultural Teachers, Agricultural Economists, Soil Scientists, Veterinarians, Entomologists, Wildlife Biologists

4. Technical Skills in Basic Schools

  • Develops in children active listening, critical thinking, and complex problem-solving skills
  • Enhances innovative abilities to apply general rules to specific problems
  • Various technologies (e.g., Internet) can enhance teaching and learning at Basic Schools

5. Visual Arts – Additional Benefits

  • Enables children to concentrate on one thing at a time
  • Enables a learner to become focused on their study and life
  • Enables learners to think and explore their imaginative and artistic skills
  • Learners learn to adapt, think, be active, and plan and execute plans
  • Enables learners to expand their knowledge and develop judgmental skills

6. Home Economics – Additional Benefits

  • Teaches management skills: financial management, resource management, time management
  • Helps children know their roles in the family effectively
  • Builds the family unit

sessions.title: Socio-Economic Importance of TVET to the Development of the Country
indicators: Discuss any three socio-economic importance of TVET to the development of the country
sessions.content:
Introduction

Technical and Vocational Education has the potential of improving the socio-economic sector of the country.

Socio-Economic Importance of TVET

1. Provision of relevant employment skills to the youth

  • The skills of a workforce make the economy of the country competitive
  • With relevant employable skills, individuals can set up their businesses and organizations
  • TVET prepares skilled labour that is readily employable in industries, other occupations, and self-employment
  • Enables individuals to pay taxes which contributes positively to national revenue mobilization
  • The more trained individuals are absorbed into the job market, the more taxes are collected to support the government's developmental agenda

2. Enhancement of problem-solving ability of citizens

  • Individuals are better placed to solve emerging challenges when they are technically enhanced
  • Training builds confidence to excel mentally in various areas of interest
  • Enhances innovative skills and brings up individuals ready to find solutions to problems
  • Skills acquired through TVET help provide good infrastructure and human resources in communities
  • Reduces or prevents disasters in the workplace

3. Social cohesion

  • Enhances nation-building agenda
  • Seeks to protect and promote health and the total well-being of families
  • TVET teaches and encourages group work and the essence of tolerance
  • Helps develop individuals with effective communication and interpersonal relations
  • Enables students and graduates to work in groups and put ideas together to come up with complete projects
  • Produces trustworthy individuals who work for the common goal of society, thereby promoting unity

4. Crime reduction

  • TVET provides knowledge and skills required in the world of work
  • Makes individuals self-reliant citizens who contribute to community development
  • Industries become productive due to rich manpower
  • Improves the quality of life of the citizenry
  • Occupies individuals for the benefit of society
  • Defeats the aged adage: 'The devil finds work for idle hands'

5. Improved productivity of industries

  • TVET prepares graduates with relevant skills related to specific industries, trades, and occupations
  • Provides broad-based knowledge that enables individuals to do critical-creative thinking
  • Highly trained personnel provide a higher output level than untrained persons
  • Waste and accidents during production are reduced
  • Increases the country's competitiveness at the global level

6. Reduces poverty

  • Provides varying job opportunities in almost every sphere of life
  • Enables individuals to make ends meet by getting necessities of life (food, clothing, shelter)

7. Life satisfaction

  • Provides varying job opportunities
  • Enables individuals to get necessities and some luxuries
  • Reduces pressures on individuals and the government

units.title: Misconceptions About TVET Courses in Ghana

sessions.title: Misconceptions About Technical Skills
indicators: Explain at least two misconceptions of Technical Education in Ghana
sessions.content:
What is a Misconception?

A misconception is a wrong notion or impression people have about an issue, a person, or an event. It normally occurs when people who are not better informed become quickly judgmental without adequate or evidential proof.

Misconceptions About Technical Skills

1. Technical Education is for dull people

  • Many Ghanaians believe technical education is meant for people who are not good academically
  • This is a wrong impression because technical education provides experience and skills for learners
  • When people know their potentialities and venture into TVET early, it helps them develop their skills and knowledge to prevent wasting time

2. Technical Schools offer inferior degrees

  • Many people think degrees awarded by technical institutions are of lower value
  • This is not true because there are many degree-earning opportunities for people who go into Technical education
  • Technical institutions offer higher degrees and one can further education to the doctorate level at KNUST

3. Job opportunities for technical students are limited

  • Some people believe Technical skills graduates are not well paid and have fewer job opportunities
  • This is a wrong idea because Engineers, Estate developers, and ICT specialists are well paid than most careers in the world

4. Technical Skill is a field for only men

  • Males used to dominate this industry probably because females are not good in mathematics, science, and technology
  • There are many young women engineers who are doing very well in the field now

5. Technical Education is very easy

  • There is a wrong idea that Technical education is easy
  • This is wrong because Technical education takes learners through detailed core concepts which are very demanding
  • Most of these require knowledge of science and mathematics to sail through

6. Technical works are dirty

  • People have the wrong impression that you get dirty when you go to work
  • Hygiene and safety is a crucial part of the study of Technical Skills
  • The dirt menace is always covered after good training and constant practice

sessions.title: Misconceptions About Visual Arts
indicators: Explain at least two misconceptions of Visual Arts Education in Ghana
sessions.content:
Misconceptions About Visual Arts

1. Visual Art is only about painting and drawing

  • Many people think Visual Arts people only paint and draw objects
  • This is a wrong idea because painting and drawing are just one aspect of Visual Arts
  • It consists of other aspects such as drama, music, dance, ceramics, graphic design, etc.

2. Visual Art is for lazy people

  • There is a wrong perception that Visual Arts is for lazy people
  • Visual Art consists of so many theory courses which require in-depth knowledge besides the extremely practical ones
  • No lazy person can do well in Visual Arts

3. Visual Art is for people with poor grades

  • There is a wrong impression that Visual Arts is for those who are academically weak
  • It is rather the creative and fast thinkers who can become an artist
  • Visual Arts involve some element of mathematical calculation (e.g., mixing colours to get the right quantity is about measurement)

4. Art Education is very costly

  • Arts Education is not as expensive as people complain
  • Even if arts education is expensive, it is worth offering because the returns and monetary gains attached to it after completion will offset any debt
  • One can easily access scholarships for higher education (e.g., Academy of Art University, San Francisco, California)

5. Arts graduates are often jobless

  • This is a misconception about Visual Art
  • There are many job opportunities for Arts people
  • Career opportunities: Painter, Sculptor, Illustrator, Graphic Designer, Photographer, Fashion Designer, Interior Designer, Package Designer, Industrial Designer, Art Therapist, Cartooning and Animation, Advertising Artist, Landscape Architect, Filmmaker, Toy Designer, Cinematographer, Makeup artiste, Ceramics, Furniture designer, Jewellery designer, Textile weaver, Archivist, Curator, Museum Technician, Art Teacher

sessions.title: Misconceptions About Home Economics
indicators: Explain at least two misconceptions about Home Economics Education in Ghana
sessions.content:
Misconceptions About Home Economics

1. Home Economics is about cooking and sewing

  • Many people have the wrong impression that Home Economics is all about cooking and sewing
  • This is not true because cooking and sewing is just one aspect of Home Economics
  • It is an applied field of study because it draws knowledge from other disciplines: agriculture, arts, biology, chemistry, mathematics, sociology, philosophy, physics, and psychology
  • It develops people to function well in the world of work and their personal lives

2. Home Economics is a female-related subject

  • There is a wrong impression that Home Economics is a field for only women
  • Though Home Economics will help you improve your home, its primary aim is to equip individuals to have a good life
  • Helps people to make informed decisions and choices in organizations and their lives
  • There are many men in Home Economics and they are doing better in their careers than women

3. Home Economics is for intellectually disadvantaged people

  • Many people have the wrong impression that Home Economics is for low achievers
  • This is not true because it is a unique field of study that has its own body of knowledge
  • It has several concepts carefully organized under related subjects: food and nutrition, housing management, clothing and textiles, and family resource management
  • It is practical-oriented and enhances creativity and innovation

4. Home Economics is very costly

  • Home Economics is a practical course that comes with constant practice, so financial obligations are there
  • The good thing is you can get your money back quickly by producing for sale
  • It is an investment worth taking because the returns are so high

sessions.title: Misconceptions About Agriculture Science
indicators: Explain at least two misconceptions about Agriculture Science Education in Ghana
sessions.content:
Misconceptions About Agriculture Science

1. Agriculture is a dirty job

  • People think agriculture is a dirty job because you cannot wear a suit to the farm or dress like an office worker
  • People who are involved in big-time commercial farming are very wealthy and can afford to purchase any form of dress and dress nicely

2. Farming is for academically disadvantaged people

  • Most people think agriculture is good for non-academics
  • This is not true because knowledge and practice of agriculture (which farming is just an aspect) is derived from the pure sciences – Biology, Chemistry, and Physics – as well as non-science courses such as Economics and Accounting

3. Farming is an outdated occupation

  • People see the agriculture sector as living in the past – the use of rudimentary tools (cutlass, hoe)
  • There are very sophisticated tools and equipment in the agriculture sector in the Western world
  • The history of the development of the human race is closely related to that of agricultural development
  • Agriculture forms an integral part of the culture of any group of people

4. People in Agriculture destroy the environment

  • People believe that agriculture harms the environment
  • This is a wrong notion because it rather enhances the capacity of individuals to adopt practices that are environmentally friendly to reduce environmental degradation (e.g., shifting cultivation)

sessions.title: Solving the Issues About Misconceptions of TVET in Ghana
indicators: Explain at least two measures that can be taken to solve the issue of misconceptions about TVET in Ghana
sessions.content:
Introduction

There are many wrong impressions about TVET in Ghana. These can be addressed through various measures.

Solutions to Misconceptions About TVET

1. Education

  • Education in the country should be refocused
  • Students should learn not only to read and write but also know what to do with the knowledge acquired after school
  • Students at the early grade (Basic level) should be taught to embrace TVET
  • Educate the public on the benefits and content of TVET to reduce or clear the misconceptions in society

2. Motivation

  • Teaching and learning must generate and sustain students' interest in TVET at the early stages of education
  • Give good guidance to enable learners to draw their career paths
  • Successful resource persons who made it through TVET must be invited often to talk to learners
  • This helps build their confidence and interest in TVET at an early stage

3. Government Policies

  • Policymakers, educators, and stakeholders must realize that the old system of education (putting much emphasis on academic brilliance and grammar) must give way to the current trend which places more emphasis on practical skills
  • Government must institute policies to revive and improve the TVET sector:
    • Abolish compulsory admission of students with very low grades into TVET programs
    • Revamp and equip Vocational and Technical schools
    • Help in educating and sensitizing the public on TVET as the only roadmap to real industrialization and development

4. Encouragement of Practical Project Work for Students

  • Students should be made to do practical projects in their area of interest as part of their education process before graduation
  • This gives them the chance to discover the benefits of TVET themselves
  • Vigorously assess learning to see if students understand the concepts taught
  • Use various techniques (e.g., projects) to help them research and have real-life experiences with the usefulness of TVET

5. Positive Teacher Attitude to TVET

  • Teachers must endeavour to understand TVET better
  • Have a good attitude towards it as other subjects
  • Encourage learners to go into it

sessions.title: TVET-Related Career Opportunities
indicators: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the various jobs, professions, trades, or work that awaits anybody who acquires TVET
sessions.content:
Career Opportunities in TVET Courses

1. Building Engineers

  • Experts in the use of technology for the design, construction, assessment, and maintenance of the built environment
  • Also called building porters or building superintendents
  • Carry out maintenance tasks along with cleaning tasks in building common areas

2. Mechanical Engineers

  • Design power-producing machines (electric generators, internal combustion engines, steam and gas turbines)
  • Design power-using machines (refrigeration and air-conditioning systems, elevators, escalators)

3. Dressmaking and Tailoring

  • Set up businesses to design and sew clothing items for people
  • Be employed to offer same services for wages or salary

4. Interior and Exterior Decoration

  • Application of the principles of design in finishing and decorating different places
  • Examples: offices, conference halls, reception, durbar grounds, weddings, and parties

5. Nutritionist

  • An expert in food and nutrition who advises people on healthy eating practices
  • Often attached to hospitals to educate people on what to eat to prevent malnutrition and other diet-related diseases

6. Fashion Designer

  • Studies fashion trends, sketches designs of clothing and accessories
  • Selects colours and fabrics
  • Oversees the final production of the design

7. Painter

  • Produces original artworks using various media: watercolour, oil, acrylics, pastels, crayon, pencil, or charcoal
  • Most painters in Ghana are self-employed, painting to fulfil the demands of individuals who buy them

8. Sculptor

  • Designs three or two-dimensional artworks
  • Methods: modelling, carving, casting, constructing, and assembling
  • Materials: clay, glass, wire, plastic, fabric, metal, wood, stone, P.O.P., sawdust, ivory, foam

9. Carpenters

  • People who work with wood
  • Make cabinets, build houses, or do other things with wood
  • Usually make very good foremen on larger jobs

10. Agribusiness Person

  • Business involves all steps required to send agricultural goods to the market
  • Includes: production, processing, and distribution

11. Electrical Engineers

  • Design and develop new electrical systems
  • Solve problems and test equipment
  • Study and apply the physics and mathematics of electricity, electromagnetism, and electronics

sessions.title: The Concept of Technology
indicators: Explain the concept of Technology
State any two types of Technology
sessions.content:
Concept of Technology

Technology is the application of scientific knowledge for practical purposes, especially in industry.

Key definitions:

  • The sum of skills, techniques, processes, and methods used for the production of goods and services, scientific investigations, or the accomplishment of an objective
  • Involves machinery and equipment developed from the application of scientific knowledge
  • TVET and Technology are closely related – every practical and theoretical aspect of TVET changes with the height of technology

Examples of Technology

1. Wood Technology

  • The trade or skills of using wood and wood products in making artefacts
  • Examples: cabinets, wood carving, joinery, carpentry, and wood turning

2. Metal Technology

  • A trade comprised of different areas of specialization: fitting and machining, fabrication and welding, foundry, and forging
  • Deals with the construction, building, manufacturing, and production of mechanical parts

3. Building Construction Technology

  • A trade used to create, design, and erect structural components
  • Includes the act of building houses, skyscrapers, and making and installing various components

4. Electronic Technology

  • A discipline that deals with the designing, creation, repair, and development of everyday devices
  • Examples: mobile phones, portable music devices, television, and computers
  • Offers the chance to produce new telecommunication, robotics, computing hardware, power, and electrical equipment

5. Electrical Technology

  • A trade that deals with installing, maintaining, and repairing electrical control, wiring, and lighting systems

6. Automotive Technology

  • Talks about engine construction, fuel and ignition systems, power trains, brakes, transmissions, electronics, and diagnostic equipment
  • An auto mechanic performs maintenance, diagnostic testing, repairs, and inspections of small trucks and cars

sessions.title: Relevance of Technology in TVET
indicators: Explain any relevance of technology in TVET
sessions.content:
Relevance of Technology in TVET

Technology is used to continuously solve problems, improve people's lives, the economy, education, etc.

Key point: Every practical and theoretical aspect of TVET changes with the height of technology.

1. Technology helps improve communications

  • Companies rely on different applications and software for communicating with others
  • Several technical aspects need to be considered before choosing communication methods
  • Examples: phone services, video conferencing, Skype, and instant messaging

2. Technology improves efficiency

  • Advancements in robotics and applied sciences help businesses facilitate their customers
  • Due to artificial intelligence, companies can now make better use of technology
  • Implementation of technology in business saves time and effort involved in employing human labour
  • Increases productivity

3. Protection against attacks

  • Online attacks are increasing at an alarming rate
  • Developers are designing cyber security such as antivirus systems to protect companies from dangerous threats
  • Companies need to protect their data by enhancing the security of their online accounts

4. Technology helps workers to work efficiently

  • The role of technology in business is expanding at a breakneck pace
  • Employees expect their supervisors to provide them with the latest equipment
  • Most recent technological equipment enables employees to complete tasks with better results and increased productivity

5. Technology adds value to teaching and learning TVET

  • Increases access for people from marginalized and disadvantaged groups
  • Example: use of phones and computers for distance education programs
  • ICT helps in re-skilling workers in the job market through distance education learning
  • Provides the climate for growth in the world of work

sessions.title: Core Value, Core Competencies, and Decision Making
indicators: Distinguish between the concept of Core Values and Core Competencies and Decision Making in TVET
sessions.content:
Decision Making

Decision Making is a choice or judgment that one or individuals have to make after thinking and talking about what is the best thing to do.

Key points:

  • The process of identifying and choosing alternatives based on the values, preferences, and beliefs of the decision-maker
  • Community members and leaders do not just get up and provide projects – they need to think and exchange ideas about what is important
  • Steps in decision making:
    • Have an awakening or sensitization – understand that your situation can be better
    • Identify your problems and accept the fact that your situation can be improved
    • Prioritize your problems – arrange them in order of importance
    • Think through very well and make a decision
    • Identify the consequences of each choice (both positive and negative)

Core Values

Core Values are those values we hold that form the foundation of our individual lives and on which we perform our work and conduct ourselves.

Key points:

  • The basic elements of how we go about our daily lives
  • Values are our way of behaving or believing what we hold most important
  • Examples: integrity, respect, responsibility, and servant leadership
  • These guiding principles dictate our behaviours and help people distinguish between what is right and wrong (e.g., honesty, respect, trustworthiness)

Core Competencies

Core Competencies are the harmonized combination of multiple resources and skills that distinguish an individual's performance from others.

Key points:

  • The defining advantages that an individual may have that distinguish that person from competitors
  • Defining characteristics that make an individual stand out from others
  • Examples of core competencies:
    • Strategic planning skills
    • Excellent organizational abilities
    • Leadership and personal management skills
    • Critical and analytical conflict resolution
    • Creative thinking
    • Evaluation skills
    • Appreciation, Truthfulness, Faith, Caring, Love
    • Communication and Collaboration
    • Critical Thinking, Creativity, and Innovation
    • Digital Literacy

Relevance of Decision Making, Core Values, and Core Competencies in TVET

  • Help to identify community problems and find solutions to them
  • Inform our thoughts, decisions, and actions
  • Help us to choose our careers or life path and do what is important to us
  • Serve as an unwavering guiding principle that dictates our behaviour
  • Help us to live authentic lives and be ourselves
  • Help to save time and make better use of resources at our workplace
  • Enhance your professional development and you can accomplish it faster
  • Right Decision Making ensures greater commitment in an organization and brings about satisfaction

sessions.title: The Relevance of Proper Value System at the Workplace
indicators: Discuss how proper value systems enhance the productivity of workers
sessions.content:
Meaning of Value

A value is anything that society cherishes. They are those principles or standards that guide the behaviour of a society.

Key point: Every society expects its members to cultivate good standards of behaviour or values. Just as society expects good behaviours from its members for proper functioning, individuals must also cultivate proper values at the workplace to sustain both the work and the employees.

Values Expected from Individuals

  • Honesty
  • Hard work
  • Dedication
  • Commitment
  • Punctuality

Relevance of Proper Value Systems

1. Honesty

  • Honest people are faithful
  • Things can be entrusted into their care
  • They can render proper accounts to their superiors
  • They do not inflate prices and pocket the difference
  • All transactions are covered by genuine documents
  • Honest behaviour puts the organization in proper shape and increases productivity and profit

2. Hard Work

  • A hard-working person is an asset to himself/herself, society, and the organization
  • Hard work helps the organization to achieve targets
  • Hard-working individuals are self-motivated and do not complain about others
  • They put out their best whether their superiors are present or not

3. Dedication

  • Dedicated people are devoted to their task
  • They work their hearts out because they love their work
  • They give their whole being to work
  • They hardly leave any room for criticism
  • Dedication leads to higher productivity and sustenance of the work

4. Punctuality

  • In Ghana, most work starts at 8 o'clock in the morning
  • A punctual worker will get ready to work on time
  • When workers develop the habit of punctuality, they can increase their productivity
  • Profits margin of the organization will also go up

5. Commitment

  • A committed person sees his/her job as something that deserves all attention
  • Bad weather or traffic congestion cannot prevent a committed person from going to work
  • Prepared to make sacrifices necessary to work until the set target is achieved
  • Prepared to do overtime for the love of the work

6. Concern for Others

  • Life is not only about solving one's problems and achieving one's goals
  • We need to extend a hand of support to others
  • Especially to people we come into contact and work with

7. Tolerance

  • Tolerance means being able to respect people's opinions or behaviours especially those you may not agree with
  • We will always have different views about issues
  • We need to respect the view of other people
  • Be patient and allow other people to express their views without attacking them

sessions.title: Meaning of Entrepreneurship
indicators: Explain Entrepreneurship
sessions.content:
Meaning of Entrepreneurship
